item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This volcano makes me think someone took Marvin's personality and implanted it into a geologic feature of the Earth. Which is awesome! Not my favorite article, but definitely amusing enough for a +1.

item.title: Re: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded: The wikiwalk/crosslink timer is running out! Do you know the perfect article to link this to? Speak up! Like Ursula, the wikiwalk needs your voices so I can marry that total hunk Eric we make the best links possible!

item.title: Staff Post: Open
item.content:encoded:
The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

Get out of my tectonic zone, mom!
*slams bedroom door*

Given that this angsty volcano can apparently listen to the news, I thought of a wonderful little reference it could whine about another volcano's activity.

So change:

SCP-1699: Haven't you been watching the news? That hurricane made landfall and killed a whole bunch of people.

Dr.B███: Yes, a tragedy.

SCP-1699: How do you think something like that makes me feel?

Dr.B███: Pardon?

to

SCP-1699: Haven't you been watching the news? That hurricane made landfall and killed a whole bunch of people.

Dr.B███: Yes, a tragedy.

SCP-1699: Even sports doesn't offer an escape anymore; have you heard about what happened to the Montreal Volcanoes? Mount Royal must think he's hot shit. How do you think something like that makes me feel?

Dr.B███: Pardon?

It may be a little wordy, but I couldn't resist such a tantalizing reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the diff between sad man and this VOLCANO is that they act different from eachother, Sad man acts like well a sad man and the volcano acts like an agnsty teen

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: He just needs someone to lava.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, I'm just gonna say that, after a quick search, I believe that this is Ruapehu in Tongariro National Park. My main evidence being that Ruapehu last erupted in 1945, and that it's in a National Park.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Coming back to reread this and looking over the comments - why does everyone seem to assume this volcano is located in Yellowstone? There's no reason even to assume it's in America, but even if it is - I'm less familiar with the geology east of the Rockies, but there is widespread past or present volcanic activity, and a fair amount of national parkland, all up and down the Pacific Coast.

Besides, depending on what you accept as canon, Yellowstone may or may not only be anomalous in that it took us freaking forever to notice it was there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This made me smile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: ~snerk~ This is great! But I think you should re-title it as "Teenaged Volcano". Seriously, sounds just like my 15 year old! ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't like how it's very similar to the Sad Man, and I don't like that the Foundation is actively trying to help it become more dangerous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Though there are similarities to 1286, I feel that this article has been presented in a unique enough manner to merit the upvotes it currently has.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm going to be nice and assume that what you wanted to say was: "This makes me think of SCP-1286 far too much."

Stolen is a pretty hefty word to throw about.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The idea is stollen from 1286. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know? I would classify this more as at least euclid because it's the Yellowstone volcano and probably will be catastrophic for the world if it will explode again; just sayin'

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It pretty much IS way too close to 1286 and I probably should be downvoting but I just can't, I eat this sort of thing up

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is 90% SCP-1286 minus the bomb part.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My biggest problem with the article is simply that I don't like the tone and approach. The idea isn't inherently bad, but the way it's written makes it seem a little immature to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was not attempting to appear aggressive, I was just making a small joke. Your opinion is no less valid for having written an article based on an odd concept - I was trying to make the point that an article is not inherintly bad for having been based on such concepts. Sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If you're warning me: I was just poking fun; it just struck me that the person who encouraged me to do an article was harassing me about it later. My feelings were not hurt, and I hope I didn't offend Lycan in any way. I was just poking fun, that's all.

If you're warning Lycan: He didn't offend me or anything; I thought it was funny. I was just a little ticked that he discounted my opinion using one of my past articles to void it. I hold nothing against him.

It might have read as me being aggressive, but I was just fooling around.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, most of the stuff that gets written around here nowadays isn't really scary either. I mean, a volcano is just a volcano; even if this isn't the Yellowstone caldera, any volcano in the world has the same chance of erupting, just without a driving consciousness behind it. You put a personality in the mix, and suddenly you're not looking at a volcano anymore, but an underdog who wants to be more than he is, and so you root for him and forget he's full of lava.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, it's the gift of the TARDIS.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Leprechauns did it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCPs almost always speak English. I blame it on magic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tsk. Not sure about the tone. In particular:

Conversation participants have described SCP-1699's disposition as "melancholy" and "bummed out", but note that despite this, it never ceases to try new avenues toward its goal.

If someone is describing an SCP as "bummed out" and it's being repeated in an official capacity, I would hope that person would be a D-class. But why would the conversation participants be the ones noting SCP-1699's intractability? And the tone of that whole "it never ceases" sounds more anecdotal than clinical. Something about the way the whole article is written, beyond the lolfoundation, is more campfire-story than official-document to me. Gotta downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: angsty volcano, I love it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I have to agree with earlier sentiments. I can't imagine "sure do whatever the volcano trying to murder millions asks you to if you feel like it" would come out of an overseer's mouth. Even if it is generally ineffective, I can't see any good reason to risk it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A volcanic eruption would blow, but it wouldn't threaten normalcy, so there's no reason to try to stop it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm beginning to question my definition of the word 'cute'

Same thing has happened to me with horror/scary. After being here for a while, mainstream media horror stuff (movies, TV series, most horror novels) just aren't as interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Aside, using an author's past works to attack their opinion of an article you like is quite lame, don't you think?

That's a greyish area. You can certainly form opinions on how "worthwhile" someone else's opinion is to you, based on your observations of their past works and comments, but actually dismissing them in comments along the lines of "your articles suck, your comments suck, you suck" counts as an ad hominem attack, which we don't allow.1

I don't think there was any real malice here, but don't do it again in the future, mkay?

Footnotes
1. As an aside, works should be judged on their own merit, not by who the author is or what the author has written before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the foundation is keeping everyone safe by constantly talking to the volcano. After all, if it stops talking and venting pressure it may actually explode as a huge eruption.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's the Little Volcano That Could! This is cute as hell.

I'm beginning to question my definition of the word 'cute'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thats like, exactly the hook for 1286.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this, I really like this for some reason, even though I feel like I should be neutral about it. Something about the depressed volcano just makes me smile on the inside. However, I do have a bit of an issue with some of the writing.
"it never ceases to try new avenues toward its goal." It may just be me, but that seems a little bit too flowery for this. Also, I agree with the people before who have raised concerns over why the scientists are helping it cause strange anomalies. Though it does make sense if they are testing it. I believe it would be more interesting/believable if you explained some of that, even in a sentence or two.
Even so, +1, because it made me happy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You know on second thought, memory is really very overrated.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I still remember that you were the one who encouraged me to do it.

EDIT: And to elaborate, I knew it was quite stupid, and only posted it because of the encouragement I got from you (I guess as a little inside joke of some kind). I believe you upvoted it as well.

Aside, using an author's past works to attack their opinion of an article you like is quite lame, don't you think?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably not… I doubt the Yellowstone Caldera would doubt its power.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You say this, but I still remember you as the author of the Russian Crusher.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is it just me, or does this read a little bit like a -J?

I like it, but mainly because it made me chuckle. I mean, come on. A stroppy volcano? That's just awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is an issue for me:

and possesses the ability to communicate vocally, utilizing steam vents to mimic speech.

What does it speak in? English? Based off this thing's age, the only language it would've heard for 95% of its lifetime would be a local Native American language, not English. (I'm assuming it's in Yellowstone.)

Now, if it's also depressed because the researchers have to translate before they can even respond, that would be interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder if it's related to SCP-1588?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still better than "potentially deadly thing we are helping for no reason whatsoever".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, that sounds too close to the "Super powerful thing that we have to make happy or else it will DESTROY US" trope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That would make this much better in my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I doubt this suggestion will be followed, but here goes.

What about flipping it so that the Volcano is depressed and we have to keep it from committing suicide, which -would- result it in blowing up? The containment procedure would consist of a team of geologist/therapists trying to talk it down?

Maybe it just late and I'm tired =D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Exactly what I was thinking. The Foundation wouldn't help an entity with any sort of power (and the volcano showed it has power, minor though it may be) to achieve its goals, not even to humor it and certainly not to make it feel better. I'm all for the Foundation being the lighter shade of grey, but this is taking it a few steps too far.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why would the Foundation have cooperated with it the first few times, before there was any experience to show that granting its requests wouldn't cause anything bad?

My immediate thought upon reading through it was that its initial requests were almost ludicrously benign, something like "play some loud rock music" or "paint the rocks pink".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think that's my favorite part; I think a big implication is that this is in Yellowstone, which is presumed by some geologists to be a "supervolcano" prime to erupt and blast half of us into space. The idea that such and event can be triggered by a sudden juvenile tantrum is fairly unsettling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
True, but part of studying an anomalous object would involve documenting the extent of its capabilities, yeah? Imagine the containment procedures for this thing when, thirty years from now, one of its little requests causes a wormhole to eat half a mountain, or blasts a hole in the moon, and it gets upgraded to Keter and the O5's get to send out all sorts of stern "No mo' this" messages to everyone concerned.

Or maybe it spawns a cute puppy that can fly, or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I downvoted because it's just a more mediocre version of the Sad Man as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Woo, more talking things which shouldn't be able to talk… Livin on the edge…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Beat me to it.

I'm not a fan of Sad Man, and that one has an actual personality. Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really can't see the Foundation helping out an anomaly which desires to be more dangerous:

  1. Why would the Foundation have cooperated with it the first few times, before there was any experience to show that granting its requests wouldn't cause anything bad?
  2. It might learn from its mistakes and figure out something that really would make it dangerous.
  3. It might simply accidentally hit on something which would make it dangerous.
  4. Maybe its only pretending to be ineffectual, in order to lull the Foundation into a false sense of security, and it will eventually get around to giving its real suggestion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Communication with SCP-1699 centers almost exclusively around its desire
to become "a more dangerous natural disaster,"

… Aaaand upvoted.

Edit: thinking about this for a minute, maybe the reason other volcanoes don't talk is because they're satisfied with their results.

Those smug bastards.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I came here to say this. It's too similar to Sad Man.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Meh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
WHAT ARE YOU DOING MR. VOLCANO?

HELLO TYRANNOSAURUS ALLAN! I'M FILLING MY WATER WITH VOLCANICITY!

EH?

RAIN FILTERS DOWN THROUGH MY HANDSOME VOLCANO ROCKS, TO CREATE A WILD MINERAL WATER!

I had to.

I wonder if there are any other sapient disasters.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, we already have one of these.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Per Roget's permission, I have fixed up the toning.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It has been fixed

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A bit of a malapropism where you used "national disaster" instead of "natural."
